Multiplex Depth for Network-Valued Data and Applications
Maoyu Zhang<sup>a,b</sup>, Linsui Deng<sup>a,c</sup>, and Wenlin Dai<sup>d</sup>
<sup>a</sup>Center for Applied Statistics, Institute of Statistics and Big Data, Renmin University of China, Beijing, China; <sup>b</sup>Goizueta Business School, Emory University,
Atlanta, GA; <sup>c</sup>School of Data Science, The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen, China; <sup>d</sup>Center for Applied Statistics, BRAIN, Institute of Statistics
and Big Data, Renmin University of China, Beijing, China
ABSTRACT
The demand for analyzing network-valued data is growing in various research areas. To facilitate robust
analysis of such data, we introduce a novel depth concept called multiplex depth, which generalizes Tukey’s
depth for multivariate data to the realm of network data. This depth is applicable to both binary and
weighted networks, enabling data ordering and outlier detection. We study the depth-related properties
of multiplex depth and establish the consistency of sample depth and depth-based median estimators.
Furthermore, we devise eficient computational algorithms to facilitate the practical implementation. To
assess the efectiveness of our approach, we conduct a comprehensive evaluation using both simulated
data and a neuroscience-derived dataset of brain networks. This evaluation takes into account crucial tasks
such as data ranking, center estimation, and outlier detection, ensuring a thorough and rigorous analysis of
our methodology. Supplementary materials for this article are available online.

1. Introduction
Network is a widely used data structure for depicting relation-
ships among objects, comprising a collection of vertices and
edges connecting the vertices. In this structure, vertices repre-
sent the objects themselves and edges represent the relationships
between them. The significance of network data has garnered
substantial attention due to its broad application in areas such
as social networks (Liu, Patacchini, and Rainone 2017; Zhu et al.
2020; Zhang, Zhang, and Dai 2024), biological networks (Zhang
and Cao 2017; Zhang et al. 2024), financial networks (Han et al.
2016; Zou et al. 2017; Accominotti, Lucena-Piquero, and Ugolini
2023), and trafic networks (Dubey and Müller 2022; Zhang et al.
2022), among others. In this article, we use the terms network
and graph interchangeably.
Most studies focus on analyzing a single network, such as
community detection (Khan and Niazi 2017; Abbe 2017; Zhang,
He, and Wang 2022), degree distribution estimation (Hay et al.
2009), or subgraph intensity estimation (Chang, Kolaczyk, and
Yao 2020). However, the focus has gradually increased on the
analysis of multiple networks, also known as network-valued
data, where the unit of data is a single network (Lovato et al.
2020, 2021). Most research into network-valued data focuses on
population modeling (Durante, Dunson, and Vogelstein 2017;
Lunagómez, Olhede, and Wolfe 2021), two-sample tests (Maugis
et al. 2020; Durante and Dunson 2018; Xia and Li 2022; Hu 2019;
Lovato et al. 2020, 2021), community detection (Barbillon et al.
2017; Rohe, Chatterjee, and Yu 2011; Paul and Chen 2020), and
other areas (Jiang, Munger, and Bunke 2001; Han et al. 2016).
The multiple network data discussed above typically consists of
network samples that share the same set of vertices, which is also
the scenario considered in our article.
A basic challenge in network analysis is that the conclu-
sions can be misleading if the network-valued data are contam-
inated by outliers. Network outliers can be roughly classified
as vertex-wise and sample-wise outliers (Hodge and Austin
2004; Zhang, Meratnia, and Havinga 2010; Gupta et al. 2014).
Vertex-wise outliers are unusual vertices within a single network
that can occur in various domains. Sample-wise outliers are
exceptional networks that exhibit unusual patterns. For example,
in patients with Alzheimer’s disease, the functional responses
or patterns observed through fMRI scans difer from those
seen in healthy aging individuals (Greicius et al. 2004). Many
studies have focused on detecting sample-wise outliers, such as
Gupta et al. (2014), who detected outliers based on network
community and graph similarity, using various measurements
(Papadimitriou, Dasdan, and Garcia-Molina 2010; Donnat and
Holmes 2018). Mining subnetwork structures comprehensively
identifies and interprets sample-wise outliers (Dang et al. 2016).
Both vertex-wise and sample-wise outliers are challenging. For
multiple networks considered in this article, we focus on the
sample-wise outliers, which motivates us to develop a robust
method with statistical depth.

sions can be misleading if the network-valued data are contam-
inated by outliers. Network outliers can be roughly classified
as vertex-wise and sample-wise outliers (Hodge and Austin
2004; Zhang, Meratnia, and Havinga 2010; Gupta et al. 2014).
Vertex-wise outliers are unusual vertices within a single network
that can occur in various domains. Sample-wise outliers are
exceptional networks that exhibit unusual patterns. For example,
in patients with Alzheimer’s disease, the functional responses
or patterns observed through fMRI scans difer from those
seen in healthy aging individuals (Greicius et al. 2004). Many

Statistical depth is a method of measuring the centrality of
data, or the extent of outlying data, in terms of their underlying
distributions, and thus generalizes the concept of order statis-
tics to multiple types of data, such as multivariate and func-
tional data (Tukey 1975; Liu 1990; López-Pintado and Romo
2009), directional or spherical data (Pandolfo, Paindaveine, and
Porzio 2018), matrix data (Chen, Gao, and Ren 2018; Paindav-
eine and Van Bever 2018), curve data (Lafaye de Micheaux,
Mozharovskyi, and Vimond 2021) and set data (Cascos, Li,
and Molchanov 2021). Zuo and Serfling (2000a) proposed a
general definition of depth for multivariate data and investigated
four key desirable depth properties. Other attempts to construct
depth in network data have mainly focused on single-network
cases. For example, Bertagnolli, Agostinelli, and De Domenico
(2020) took a novel approach and equipped projected Tukey
depth with a network-embedding metric to identify the central-
ity of vertices, while Raj et al. (2017) extended the band depth
to path ensembles within a particular network to systematically
visualize the paths. A natural question, then, is how to establish
a depth function for multiple networks that combines with the
network structure and extends the depth properties.

and Molchanov 2021). Zuo and Serfling (2000a) proposed a

four key desirable depth properties. Other attempts to construct

cases. For example, Bertagnolli, Agostinelli, and De Domenico
(2020) took a novel approach and equipped projected Tukey
depth with a network-embedding metric to identify the central-
ity of vertices, while Raj et al. (2017) extended the band depth
to path ensembles within a particular network to systematically
visualize the paths. A natural question, then, is how to establish
a depth function for multiple networks that combines with the
network structure and extends the depth properties.
We propose a depth notion for network-valued data, named
multiplex depth. This notion extends Tukey’s depth to the
domain of multiple networks, enabling eficient outlier detection
and network ordering. The multiplex depth framework is
applicable to all unweighted/weighted and undirected/directed
networks, facilitated by eficient computational algorithms.
The work marks an initial attempt to extend Tukey’s concept
of data depth into the realm of multiple networks, although
it acknowledges some constraints on its applicability. Our
contribution is multi-fold. First, we introduce an extension
of the depth concept into the realm of multiple networks,
incorporating both global and local network structures. Second,
we establish the properties of the proposed depth measures and
demonstrate the consistency of the depth-based center with
the population center. Additionally, we introduce a structure
weight associated with the depth, derived from the network
structure, which significantly enhances the interpretability of
the ranking outcomes. Lastly, we validate the efectiveness of
our approach through both simulations and a study on brain
connectivity, encompassing data ranking, center estimation, and
outlier detection.
As a primary step in defining depth for network data, our
article considers a collection of independent network samples
defined over a common set of vertices. These assumptions are
well satisfied by networks from various research areas, such as
bioinformatics (Zhang and Cao 2017) and neuroscience (Zhou
and Li 2014; Zhang et al. 2024), while could be violated by
networks from some other areas, for example social networks
and economic networks, where the vertex set may vary among
samples. We leave the extension of our framework to more
general cases as future work.
The remainder of the article is organized as follows. Section 2
describes a generalized depth notion for networks, termed (sub-
graph) multiplex depth, focusing on the whole network first and
then gradually shifting to subgraphs. We thoroughly investigate
the edgewise multiplex depth function as an important special
case and introduce the structure weights tailored for network
analysis. In Section 3, we examine the theoretical properties of
edgewise multiplex depth and derive the consistency between
the depth-based empirical center and population center. In Sec-
tion 4, we evaluate the performance of the proposed depth
for unweighted and weighted networks, in terms of data rank-
ing, center estimation, and outlier detection. In Section 5, we
apply the proposed depth to analyze an electroencephalography
dataset via tasks of center estimation, outlier detection and a
two-sample test. Section 6 provides a discussion.

recommend identifying several, rather than a single, deepest
network to comprehensively analyze the property of network
ensemble; see Section 5 for details. The network structure pro-
vides flexible weights according to practical necessities. If the
weights of interested structures are continuous with respect to
edge weights, the depth properties and consistency still hold,
according to the continuous mapping theorem.
Finally, our theoretical results can be naturally extended to
sparse weighted networks with consistently existent edges. We
first define a subgraph indicator as an unweighted network
where an edge exists between two vertices if it consistently
appears in these sparse weighted networks. The depth properties
and convergence theories for weighted networks can then be
adapted to sparse weighted networks by restricting them to this

The independent network samples can be generated from many
unweighted network models, such as the Erdos-Rényi model˝
(Gilbert 1959), stochastic block model (Wang and Wong 1987;
Zhang, Sun, and Li 2020), and a collection of noisy networks
(Chang, Kolaczyk, and Yao 2020); meanwhile, weighted con-
nectivity networks for each subject in brain neuroscience are
independent samples (Brands
Zhang, Sun, and Li 2023).
A parallel result for gMD is given in Theorem S3, where the
result for weighted networks is a direct corollary of Theorem 2
in López-Pintado and Romo (2009).
We require the structure weight
w _ { i j }
to be strictly positive
to ensure the identifiability of the central network. The con-
dition of unique maximization is standard when identifying a
representative element for a distribution or ensemble (Ziezold
1977; Zuo and Serfling 2000b; López-Pintado and Romo 2006),
which is guaranteed by the maximality at center for weighted
(Theorem 1(a)) and unweighted networks (Theorem S1(a)). In
a related study for networks, Kolaczyk et al. (2020) discussed
the geometric requirements for the uniqueness of the Fréchet
mean corresponding to the unlabeled and undirected weighted
networks. On the other hand, when
\mathrm { e M D } _ { p r } ^ { K } ( \cdot ; W )
is maximized
at several networks, named a central network set, the estimated
center will locate into the set almost surely. In practice, we
recommend identifying several, rather than a single, deepest
network to comprehensively analyze the property of network
ensemble; see Section 5 for details. The network structure pro-
vides flexible weights according to practical necessities. If the
weights of interested structures are continuous with respect to
edge weights, the depth properties and consistency still hold,
according to the continuous mapping theorem.
Finally, our theoretical results can be naturally extended to
sparse weighted networks with consistently existent edges. We
first define a subgraph indicator as an unweighted network
where an edge exists between two vertices if it consistently
appears in these sparse weighted networks. The depth properties
and convergence theories for weighted networks can then be
adapted to sparse weighted networks by restricting them to this
subgraph indicator.

Vertices with higher degrees are more influential, which

The important module in the PA model is the subgraph

ranks and compare eMD and Ham using the average ranks of the
networks with or without the important modules. The results
of the two models are similar and both confirm the usefulness
of the structure weight. We find almost no diference between
the ranking results of the two groups with the equal weight for
either Ham or eMD. With the vertex weight, the ranking results
reveal weak diferences for eMD and remain comparable for
Ham. With the edge weight, the diferences between the two
groups are significant for both methods. The networks without
the important module are assigned with smaller rankings,
confirming the assumption that they outlie to a greater degree.
These results indicate that the edge weight is more efective
than the other options in capturing the important module of
interest. For other practical implementations, the weight should
be adapted for the specific tasks.

data. This dataset is derived from an empirical investigation
conducted to explore the EEG manifestations of the genetic pre-
disposition to alcoholism (http://kdd.ics.uci.edu/datasets/eeg/

(2010), Hung and Wang (2013) and Zhou and Li (2014).
Following their practice, our study concentrates on com-
puting the average values across all trials conducted under the
single-stimulus condition for each individual. As a result, this
yields a spatial-temporal matrix of dimensions 256×64 for each
subject. To make the data compatible with our method, we ini-
tially excluded three electrodes that lacked detailed descriptions,
retaining a total of 61 electrodes with specific information, as
listed in Table S1 of the supplementary material. Therefore, we

The brain functional connectivity is commonly modeled
using graph edges with correlation coeficients (Rubinov and
Sporns 2010) as weights, which signify the strength of connec-
tivity between vertices. While binarizing these coeficients can
indicate connection existence, it may overlook crucial details
and the analysis outcomes depend on the chosen threshold
(Colon-Perez et al. 2016; Ahmadi, Fatemizadeh, and Nasrabad
2020). Additionally, in eMD method, using a binary data
structure may result in some ties. Therefore, drawing inspiration
from the key idea of eMD, we prefer to integrate edge weights
into our approach if we can directly obtain weighted networks
from the real data. Specifically, we determine connectivity in
each individual by computing Pearson correlations between
samples from a pair of regions. For subject l, we set the edge
weight of the connectivity network as
\dot { e ( i , j ) } = \mathrm { c o r } ( B _ { . i } ^ { ( l ) } , B _ { . j } ^ { ( l ) } )
\begin{array} { r c l } { i , j } & { = } & { 1 , \ldots , 6 1 } \end{array}
, which is commonly employed in existing
neuroscience literature (Power et al. 2011). Finally, we obtain 122
networks classified into two groups consisting of 77 networks
for the alcoholism and 45 for the control.
First, we use eMD to find the central and outlying networks
for each group, to investigate the diversity within and between
groups. Figure 6 plots the heatmaps of the connectivity networks
of two groups, with the three largest and smallest eMDs. For
both groups, the main active regions of centers are in the frontal
related and parietal related regions. This is consistent with exist-
ing findings that frontal and parietal regions are working in
object recognition tasks (Castelli et al. 2000; Pennick and Kana
2012). To further visualize the EEG electrode positions, two
groups of vertex strength heatmaps with three maximal eMDs
centers are presented in Figure S5 in the supplementary material,
which further supports the findings in Figure 6.
To the contrast, the outliers in both groups are extremely
active in all regions. To verify the correctness of the detected
outliers, we map the two group of subjects onto the Euclidean
two-dimensional space via multidimensional scaling (MDS).
MDS begins with computing a dissimilarity matrix based on the
input data, followed by optimization that aims to minimize the
discrepancy between the pairwise dissimilarities in the original
data and those in the reduced-dimensional space, so as to find a
lower-dimensional representation that best preserves the origi-
nal pairwise dissimilarities. We apply eMD to rank the network
ensembles from two groups and visualize the ranking results of
each network using MDS through the cmdscale function from
R package stat as shown in Figure 7. As shown, most outliers
are isolated from the bulk of the data cloud, and the remaining
samples display a more reasonable pattern. The performance
reveals that eMD is useful and favorable in practical applications.
For weighted networks, the strength of a vertex is defined
as the sum of the connected edge weights. Its distribution is a
powerful tool to characterize the network properties. In order to
quantify the strength of a vertex i, we define it as the sum of the
absolute values of the weights of the connected edges, denoted
as
d _ {i} = \sum_ {j \neq i} | A _ {i j} |, \quad i = 1, \dots , N.
We construct the empirical strength distribution of a network
ensemble by pooling up the strength sequences for all samples.
Specifically, we estimate the density of vertex strength by kernel
smoothing, which employs a Gaussian kernel with bandwidth
chosen via the default method specified by the density function
from R package stats. Figure 8 shows the 10% central region, 10%

Then, we propose a two-sample rank test for network data
with eMD by extending the concept of Liu and Singh (1993);
see the detailed procedure in Section SIV of the supplementary
material. We classify the networks of alcoholism and control as
two groups and test the statistical significance of the diference
between them with the proposed test. The p-value is 0.00218,
suggesting that the EEG networks of alcoholism and control
are significantly diferent. Further, the removal of outliers from
both groups of samples is necessary to ensure the reliability and
validity of the analysis. Outliers can significantly influence the
test results and may lead to biased interpretations, making their
exclusion essential for accurate statistical comparisons. Thus, we
remove the 5% most outlying samples of each group and then
re-perform the two-sample test on the remaining samples. The
p-value becomes 0.00186, smaller than the previous one, which
reinforces the fact that the two sample groups are statistically
significantly diferent. This is in line with the results in Li, Kim,

6. Discussion
This study provides a robust description of a collection of net-
works built on common vertices. We define depth notions for
network-valued data by incorporating global and local network
structures. These are widely applicable to various networks (e.g.,
unweighted or weighted, directed or undirected). The depth
values provide a central-outward ranking for a network col-
lection, and consequently a series of rank-based description
statistics, such as medians and outliers. The structure weight is
included during the construction of proposed depths to further
account for the structural information. In addition, we inves-
tigate their theoretical properties in terms of data depth and
establish the consistency of depths and their induced medians.
Fast algorithms are developed to ease practical implementation.
The robustness of the new depths is evaluated through extensive
simulation studies and a real example.
The proposed depths can be regarded as a nonparametric
modeling of a network data ensemble. We have demonstrated
their applications in several canonical tasks, such as robust
center estimation, outlier detection, and a two-sample test. The
new depths are also applicable to network data classification
and clustering (Relión et al. 2019), change point detection for
dynamic networks (Huang et al. 2020), robust community detec-
tion (Martinet et al. 2020), and link prediction (Martínez, Berzal,
and Cubero 2017).
Several future research directions emerge from this work.
First, the proposed depth notions work well for labeled net-
works, and investigating their extensions to unlabeled networks
is a natural next step. Second, it is possible to extend our model
framework to encompass networks from diferent sets, wherein
variations in the number of vertices among observations are
frequent occurrences in dynamic networks. In addition, explor-
ing the depth properties for sparse weighted networks with
inconsistently existent edges is an interesting topic. Another
direction is to combine external information (e.g., vertex-level
or sample-level covariates) with the network itself. Further efort
is required to derive the theoretical properties for a general sub-
graph multiplex depth and develop an eficient computational
algorithm.
